09 Advanced TypeScript — The Master Class

← Back to lessons

This is where TypeScript becomes a language unto itself. Template literal types, branded types, discriminated unions at scale, and module augmentation. These patterns separate library authors from library consumers.

Template Literal Types

TypeScript can manipulate string types at the type level, just like template literals manipulate strings at runtime:

// Basic template literal type
type Greeting = `Hello, ${string}`;
const a: Greeting = "Hello, Trevor";   // OK
const b: Greeting = "Goodbye, Trevor"; // Error!

// Union distribution in template literals
type Color = "red" | "blue";
type Size = "sm" | "lg";
type ClassName = `${Color}-${Size}`;
// "red-sm" | "red-lg" | "blue-sm" | "blue-lg"

// CSS-like utility
type CSSProperty = "margin" | "padding";
type Direction = "top" | "right" | "bottom" | "left";
type CSSKey = `${CSSProperty}-${Direction}`;
// "margin-top" | "margin-right" | ... | "padding-left"
// All 8 combinations, automatically

String Manipulation Types

// Built-in string manipulation utility types
type A = Uppercase<"hello">;      // "HELLO"
type B = Lowercase<"HELLO">;      // "hello"
type C = Capitalize<"hello">;     // "Hello"
type D = Uncapitalize<"Hello">;   // "hello"

// Combine with template literals for powerful patterns
type EventName<T extends string> = `on${Capitalize<T>}`;
type E = EventName<"click">;      // "onClick"
type F = EventName<"change">;     // "onChange"

// Generate event handler types from a props interface
type PropEvents<T> = {
  [K in keyof T as K extends string ? EventName<K> : never]:
    (value: T[K]) => void;
};

interface FormFields {
  name: string;
  age: number;
}

type FormEvents = PropEvents<FormFields>;
// { onName: (value: string) => void; onAge: (value: number) => void }

This is how libraries like Prisma work. Prisma reads your database schema and generates types like findUnique, findMany, create, update for each model. Template literal types + mapped types + conditional types = fully type-safe database queries generated from a schema.

Discriminated Unions at Scale

Lesson 07 introduced the basic pattern. Here's how to use it for complex state machines:

// A state machine for an article workflow
type ArticleState =
  | { status: "draft"; content: string; author: string }
  | { status: "review"; content: string; author: string; reviewer: string }
  | { status: "published"; content: string; author: string; publishedAt: Date; url: string }
  | { status: "archived"; content: string; author: string; archivedAt: Date };

// Type-safe transitions
function submitForReview(article: Extract<ArticleState, { status: "draft" }>, reviewer: string):
  Extract<ArticleState, { status: "review" }> {
  return { ...article, status: "review", reviewer };
}

function publish(article: Extract<ArticleState, { status: "review" }>):
  Extract<ArticleState, { status: "published" }> {
  return {
    ...article,
    status: "published",
    publishedAt: new Date(),
    url: `/articles/${encodeURIComponent(article.content.slice(0, 50))}`,
  };
}

// Exhaustive switch with never check
function getStatusMessage(article: ArticleState): string {
  switch (article.status) {
    case "draft":     return `Draft by ${article.author}`;
    case "review":    return `Under review by ${article.reviewer}`;
    case "published": return `Live at ${article.url}`;
    case "archived":  return `Archived on ${article.archivedAt.toISOString()}`;
    default:
      const _exhaustive: never = article;
      return _exhaustive;
  }
}

The never trick: If you add a new status to ArticleState but forget to handle it in the switch, TypeScript will error at the never assignment. This guarantees exhaustive handling at compile time. It's a simple pattern with enormous safety benefits.

Branded Types

Sometimes two values have the same runtime type (both string) but represent different things. Branded types add compile-time distinction:

// The problem: all IDs are strings, easy to mix up
function getUser(userId: string) { ... }
function getArticle(articleId: string) { ... }

const id = "abc-123";
getUser(id);      // OK
getArticle(id);   // Also OK — but this is a USER id, not an article id!

// The solution: branded types
type UserId = string & { readonly __brand: "UserId" };
type ArticleId = string & { readonly __brand: "ArticleId" };

// Smart constructors
function UserId(id: string): UserId {
  return id as UserId;
}

function ArticleId(id: string): ArticleId {
  return id as ArticleId;
}

function getUser(userId: UserId) { ... }
function getArticle(articleId: ArticleId) { ... }

const userId = UserId("user-123");
const articleId = ArticleId("article-456");

getUser(userId);       // OK
getUser(articleId);     // Error! ArticleId is not assignable to UserId
getUser("raw-string"); // Error! string is not assignable to UserId

Branded types are compile-time fiction. At runtime, UserId is just a string. The __brand property doesn't exist in memory. It's a phantom type that TypeScript uses to prevent mixing up values that look the same at runtime but mean different things. It's like putting color-coded labels on identical-looking file folders.

Validated Branded Types

type Email = string & { readonly __brand: "Email" };
type NonEmptyString = string & { readonly __brand: "NonEmptyString" };

function Email(input: string): Email {
  if (!/^[^\s@]+@[^\s@]+\.[^\s@]+$/.test(input)) {
    throw new Error(`Invalid email: ${input}`);
  }
  return input as Email;
}

function NonEmptyString(input: string): NonEmptyString {
  if (input.trim().length === 0) {
    throw new Error("String must not be empty");
  }
  return input as NonEmptyString;
}

// Now your functions can REQUIRE validated input
function sendEmail(to: Email, subject: NonEmptyString) { ... }

// Callers must validate first — the brand is proof of validation
sendEmail(Email("trevor@example.com"), NonEmptyString("Hello"));

Module Augmentation & Declaration Merging

You can extend third-party types without modifying their source code:

// Add custom properties to Express Request
declare module "express" {
  interface Request {
    userId?: string;
    sessionToken?: string;
  }
}

// Now TypeScript knows about these properties
app.use((req, res, next) => {
  req.userId = "trevor-123";  // No error
  next();
});

Augmenting Global Types

// Add to the global Window type
declare global {
  interface Window {
    analytics: {
      track(event: string, data?: Record<string, unknown>): void;
    };
  }
}

window.analytics.track("page_view", { page: "/articles" });

Augmenting Next.js Types

// Extend Next.js env types for type-safe environment variables
declare namespace NodeJS {
  interface ProcessEnv {
    DATABASE_URL: string;
    API_KEY: string;
    NODE_ENV: "development" | "production" | "test";
  }
}

// Now process.env.DATABASE_URL is typed as string, not string | undefined
const db = connect(process.env.DATABASE_URL);  // No null check needed

Declaration merging only works with interface, not type. This is the one scenario where the interface vs type distinction truly matters. Third-party libraries expose interfaces specifically so consumers can augment them.

Advanced Patterns

Type-Safe Builder Pattern

class QueryBuilder<Selected extends string = never> {
  private fields: string[] = [];
  private table = "";

  from(table: string): this {
    this.table = table;
    return this;
  }

  select<F extends string>(field: F): QueryBuilder<Selected | F> {
    this.fields.push(field);
    return this as any;
  }

  execute(): Promise<Record<Selected, unknown>[]> {
    const sql = `SELECT ${this.fields.join(", ")} FROM ${this.table}`;
    // Execute and return typed results
    return fetch(`/api/query?sql=${sql}`).then(r => r.json());
  }
}

const results = await new QueryBuilder()
  .from("articles")
  .select("title")
  .select("author")
  .execute();

// results is Record<"title" | "author", unknown>[]
results[0].title;   // OK
results[0].body;    // Error: 'body' was not selected

Recursive Types

// JSON type (recursive)
type JSONValue =
  | string
  | number
  | boolean
  | null
  | JSONValue[]
  | { [key: string]: JSONValue };

// Deep partial (recursive mapped type)
type DeepPartial<T> = {
  [K in keyof T]?: T[K] extends object ? DeepPartial<T[K]> : T[K];
};

// Deep readonly
type DeepReadonly<T> = {
  readonly [K in keyof T]: T[K] extends object ? DeepReadonly<T[K]> : T[K];
};

interface Config {
  db: { host: string; port: number; };
  cache: { ttl: number; };
}

type PartialConfig = DeepPartial<Config>;
// { db?: { host?: string; port?: number }; cache?: { ttl?: number } }

Knowledge Check

1. What type does this resolve to?

type Route = `/${"users" | "posts"}/${"list" | "detail"}`;
"/users/list" | "/users/detail" | "/posts/list" | "/posts/detail". Template literal types distribute over unions, producing all 4 combinations.

2. Why do branded types use string & { __brand: "X" } instead of a class?

Because the brand needs to be invisible at runtime. A class would add runtime overhead and change the value's prototype. The intersection with a phantom property exists only in the type system (TypeScript's structural typing means the __brand property is checked at compile time but never needs to exist at runtime). The value remains a plain string.

3. When does the never exhaustiveness check catch bugs?

When you add a new variant to a discriminated union but forget to handle it in a switch statement. After all known cases are handled, the default branch narrows the type to never. If a new case exists that isn't handled, the type in the default branch won't be never anymore, and the assignment to const _: never = value will fail at compile time.

Exercise: Build a Type-Safe Router

Create a type-safe routing system using template literal types:

// 1. Define route patterns with params
type Routes = {
  "/articles": {};
  "/articles/:id": { id: string };
  "/articles/:id/comments/:commentId": { id: string; commentId: string };
};

// 2. Build ExtractParams<T> that infers params from the path
type ExtractParams<T extends string> = ...
// ExtractParams<"/articles/:id"> → { id: string }

// 3. Create a navigate function
function navigate<P extends keyof Routes>(
  path: P,
  params: Routes[P]
): string { ... }

navigate("/articles/:id", { id: "123" });  // OK
navigate("/articles/:id", {});              // Error: missing id
navigate("/articles", { id: "123" });       // Error: no params expected

Bonus: Use infer to build ExtractParams automatically from the path pattern string.
